critique.summary: The video by Corridor Crew discusses a longstanding visual effects (VFX) challenge related to green screen technology, specifically the transparency and chroma key problems. The creator explains how this problem has persisted for decades and introduces a new approach involving neural networks and procedural generation to address it.
mainPoints: Green screens have inherent issues, particularly with transparency and chroma keying.
Previous solutions have not fully resolved these problems.
The video presents a new method using neural networks and procedural generation to improve green screen compositing.
The creator claims it took 30 years to develop this solution.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about green screen challenges and chroma key problems are well-known in the VFX industry and accurately described. The use of neural networks and procedural generation is a current and plausible approach to improving compositing techniques. However, the claim of taking 30 years to solve the problem is likely a personal or hyperbolic statement rather than a verifiable fact. The video appears to be an informed discussion from a reputable VFX team but does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives.
considerations: The video does not provide independent verification or external expert opinions on the new method's effectiveness.
It lacks detailed technical data or comparative results against existing green screen solutions.
The claim of a definitive solution to green screen problems should be viewed as a significant advancement rather than a complete industry-wide resolution.
The content is from a popular YouTube channel known for VFX content but not a traditional journalistic source.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around advancements and challenges in green screen keying technology, including AI-driven solutions, traditional techniques, and practical on-set issues. Commenters share insights on technical methods, software tools, and production experiences, highlighting both innovations and ongoing limitations.

comments.author: dylan604
comments.text:
The sad thing about this is the problems encountered during post from the production team saying "fix it post" during the shoot. I've been on set for green screen shoots where the lighting was not done properly. I watched the gaffer walk across the set taking readings from his meter before saying the lighting was good. I flip on the waveform and told him it was not even (which never goes down well when camera dept tells the gaffer it's not right). He put up an argument, went back and took measurements again before repeating it was good. I flipped the screen around and showed him where it was obviously not even. A third set of meter readings and he starts adjust lights. Once the footage was in post, the fx team commented about how easy the keys were because of the even lighting.

The problem is that the vast majority of people on set have no clue what is going on in post. To the point, when the budget is big enough, a post supervisor is present on production days to give input so "fixing it in post" is minimized. When there is no budget, you'll see situations just like in the first 30 seconds of TFA's video. A single lamp lighting the background so you can easily see the light falling off and the shadows from wrinkles where the screen was just pulled out of the bag 10 minutes before shooting. People just don't realize how much light a green screen takes. They also fail to have enough space so they can pull the talent far enough off the wall to avoid the green reflecting back onto the talent's skin.

TL;DR They solved something to make post less expensive because they cut corners during production.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, experience-based explanation of on-set lighting challenges and their impact on post-production, reflecting accurate industry practices.

comments.author: Springtime
comments.text: In an earlier video they made a couple years back about Disney's sodium vapor technique Paul Debevec suggested he was considering creating a dataset using a similar premise: filming enough perfectly masked references to be able to train models to achieve better keying. So it was interesting seeing Corridor tackle this by instead using synthetic data.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately references known research directions in VFX keying and contrasts approaches, showing informed understanding.

comments.author: qingcharles
comments.text:
I use the Adobe version of this in Photoshop every day and I assumed that Adobe solved this the same way, but used professionals to cut out the subjects from the backgrounds then fed both versions into their AI.

Since they added it a year or so ago it has been game-changing. I'm cutting out portraits every day and having a magical tool that cuts out the subject with perfect hair cut out with a single click is sci-fi.

Here's a demo of Photoshop's tool:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SNVJN6PKeGQ

(the other magical Photoshop tool is the one that removes reflections from windows, which is even more insane when you reverse it and tell it you only want the reflection and not what's on the other side of the glass)
comments.analysis: The description aligns with Adobe's recent AI-powered selection tools, providing a realistic user perspective on their effectiveness.

comments.author: jayd16
comments.text:
As far as alternatives, I wonder if anyone has tried a screen that cycles through colors in a known sequence. Using this modulating-color screen, it might actually be easier to separate the subject because you get around the "green shirt over green screen" problem. You might even be able to use a time sampling to correct the light cast on the subject from the screen as you would have a full spectrum of response.

I could also imagine using polarized light as the backdrop as well.
comments.analysis: This is a creative and plausible technical suggestion, though it lacks evidence of practical implementation.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
From ~04:10 till 05:00 they talk about sodium-vapor lights and how Disney has the exclusive rights to use it. From what I read the knowledge on how to make them is a trade secret, so it's not patented. Seems weird that it would be hard to recreate something from the 1950's.

I also wonder how many hours were wasted by people who had to use inferior technology because Disney kept it secret. Cutting out animals and objects from the background 1 frame at a time seems so mindnumbingly boring.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly distinguishes trade secrets from patents and reasonably speculates on the impact of secrecy on industry practices.

comments.author: superjan
comments.text: Watched this a few days ago. The video is light on technical details, except maybe that they used CGI to generate training data.
comments.analysis: A brief but accurate observation about the video's content, noting the use of synthetic data for training.

comments.author: MrVitaliy
comments.text: Anyone tried using lidar and just cut/measure distance to the object?
comments.analysis: A relevant and insightful question suggesting an alternative technology that could aid in segmentation.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: There's still a bug: the glass with water does not distort the checker pattern in the background at 24:12.
comments.analysis: This comment points out a specific visual inconsistency in the video, indicating careful observation.

comments.author: vsviridov
comments.text: The community has managed to drastically lower hardware requirements, but so far I think only Nvidia cards are supported, so as an AMD owner I'm still missing out :(
comments.analysis: Reflects a common hardware compatibility issue in AI and graphics software, likely accurate.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: I'm a software engineer that, like the vast majority of you, uses AI/agents in my workflow every day. That being said, I have to admit that it feels a little weird to hear someone who does not write code say that they built something, without even mentioning that they had an agent build it (unless I missed that).
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective opinion about crediting AI contributions, relevant but less factual.
